prompts: The double-slit experiment demonstrates the wave-particle duality of light and other particles. When coherent light passes through two closely spaced slits, it diffracts, creating two overlapping wavefronts. These overlapping waves interfere, producing a pattern of bright and dark fringes on a screen. Bright fringes occur where waves are in phase (constructive interference), and dark fringes occur where they are out of phase (destructive interference). If particles like electrons are used, they form the same interference pattern, suggesting wave behavior, even when particles pass through the slits one at a time, highlighting the probabilistic nature of quantum mechanics.
